summary: Gruber argues that the rumored OLED iPhone will be priced significantly higher than existing models — likely $1,200 or more — because Apple faces fundamental supply constraints on OLED displays that prevent manufacturing at typical iPhone scale. He reasons that the existence of iPhone 7S and 7S Plus models alongside the OLED phone only makes sense if the OLED model occupies a new premium tier, since otherwise Apple would simply make the OLED phone the standard model. Drawing on Ming-Chi Kuo's supply chain reports, he builds a case that pricing must be high enough to ensure tens of millions of buyers still choose the LCD models. He compares this to Honda creating Acura and Toyota creating Lexus — a premium tier that doesn't cannibalize the mainstream product. He acknowledges the risk that a premium OLED iPhone could dampen excitement for the 7S models without converting those customers to the higher price point.
key_points: iPhone pricing isn't just about component costs and margins — it's constrained by what Apple can manufacture at a scale of roughly 1 million units per day
The existence of both LCD (7S/7S Plus) and OLED models only makes strategic sense if the OLED model occupies a distinctly higher price tier
Supply constraints on OLED displays mean Apple literally cannot produce enough units to meet demand at current iPhone price points, so higher prices serve as a demand valve
A premium iPhone tier is strategically necessary to prevent competitors from leapfrogging Apple with components that can't yet be produced at massive scale
The biggest risk is a 'neither fish nor fowl' consumer sentiment where people reject the 7S as boring but refuse to pay $1,200+ for the premium model
money_quotes: New iPhones aren't defined by what Apple can build for a certain price, but by what Apple can make for a certain price at a certain incredibly high quantity.
The iPhone's scale — particularly the incredible popularity of the new high-end models each year — is Apple's biggest financial strength but simultaneously its biggest potential strategic weakness.
When supply is constrained and demand is high, prices go higher. The higher prices alleviate demand.

If the 'iPhone Pro' or 'iPhone Edition' or whatever it is that Apple is going to call this phone starts at $800 or even $900, who is going to buy an iPhone 7S or 7S Plus? Not enough people, that's who.

If you want to argue that Apple should never create an iPhone with a higher starting price than what we have today, you're implicitly arguing that Apple should never put any components into a new iPhone that can't be made at iPhone 7 scale.
I don't want a 7S because it's boring, but I don't want to spend $1,200 on an iPhone Pro/Edition because that's too much for a phone, no matter how nice it is.

In the same way it made sense for Honda and Toyota to create their Acura and Lexus divisions to sell higher-end cars without eroding the value or popularity of their best-selling Accords and Camrys, it makes sense for Apple to create a premium tier for the iPhone.
key_insight: Apple's OLED iPhone must be priced as a premium tier above existing models because supply constraints make it impossible to produce at traditional iPhone scale, and lower pricing would cannibalize the LCD models Apple needs to sell in massive quantities.
